title: StandUp4AI: A New Multilingual Dataset for Humor Detection in Stand-up Comedy Videos
research_questions: Can a large multilingual stand-up comedy video dataset support computational modeling of humor and audience laughter across seven languages?
Can complementary timestamp errors from Whisper and WhisperX be used to discover laughter events missed by an off-the-shelf laughter detector?
Does framing humor detection as word-level sequence labeling, rather than binary sequence classification, provide usable baselines for predicting audience laughter during stand-up performances?
tasks: sequence labeling of laughter after words

datasets_used.name: StandUp4AI candidate laughter annotation subset
datasets_used.size: 376 outburst candidates on 50 videos; 208 real outbursts of laughter

models_evaluated: Omine et al. (2024) laughter detection model

methods_proposed.name: ASR-Based Automatic Laughter Detection
methods_proposed.summary: The method exploits complementary timestamp errors from Whisper and WhisperX around laughter: Whisper tends to merge laughter duration with the next word, while WhisperX tends to merge it with the previous one. Intersections between the two transcripts are used to insert candidate laughter segments, which are then filtered with a Random Forest classifier using acoustic features.

methods_proposed.name: Word-level laughter prediction as sequence labeling
methods_proposed.summary: Instead of treating humor detection as binary sequence classification, each word is labeled to indicate whether laughter occurs right after it or continues through it. This allows models to predict when laughter should start and stop across a performance rather than only at the end of a sequence.

humor_theories.detail: The introduction motivates humor as emerging when a situation surprisingly deviates from expectations, with setup, punchline, and tags used to describe joke structure.

evaluation_methods.detail: The authors manually annotated 70 videos, 10 per language, with laughter timestamps at 0.1 seconds using the audio file and Audacity; ASR outputs were manually checked.
The authors manually annotated candidate laughter segments detected in 50 videos as real laughter or other events.
The Random Forest candidate laughter classifier was evaluated with precision, recall, and F1.
The full laughter detection system was evaluated against the manually annotated test set using Intersection over Union with threshold 0.2, reporting precision, recall, and F1.
Word-level sequence labeling models were evaluated with classification metrics, including F1 scores by language and average F1.

headline_results.finding: StandUp4AI contains 3,617 stand-up videos across seven languages.
The ASR-based candidate extraction produced 376 outburst candidates on 50 videos, of which 208 were real laughter events not previously detected by the off-the-shelf model.
headline_results.value: 376 candidates; 208 real outbursts; approximately 3 new outbursts of laughter per video

headline_results.finding: The Random Forest candidate laughter classifier achieved the best reported class F1 of 0.85 for laughter and macro F1 of 0.83.
On the manually annotated test set, the filtered ASR-based laughter detection method outperformed the Omine et al. (2024) off-the-shelf detector.
For word-level sequence labeling on the manually annotated test set, the multilingual model trained on enhanced laughter labels achieved the best average F1.
On the automatic test set, the multilingual enhanced model achieved the best average F1.

key_findings: The paper introduces StandUp4AI, a multilingual stand-up comedy video dataset with 3,617 videos, 334.2 hours, 2,887,402 words, and 128,194 laughter labels across seven languages.
The ASR-based method found 208 real outbursts of laughter among 376 candidates on 50 videos that were not previously detected by the off-the-shelf laughter detection model.
Filtering ASR-derived laughter candidates with a Random Forest improved manually evaluated laughter detection from F1 0.51 for Omine et al. (2024) to F1 0.58 at IoU threshold 0.2.
For word-level laughter prediction, the multilingual model trained with enhanced labels achieved Avg. F1 42.4, compared with 42.2 for multilingual raw labels and 39.4 for monolingual enhanced models.
The paper releases additional multimodal features, including Action Units, poses, and camera angle changes, but the reported baseline experiments use unimodal text models.
limitations: The humor detection experiments focus only on unimodal textual models; multimodal models are left for future work.
The paper does not model different intensities of laughter because of significant variability in acoustic intensity across collected videos.
The authors state that a more thorough analysis of ASR errors would be beneficial, especially for dialectal language, slang, and vulgarity.
The dataset relies on YouTube videos that may be deleted.
The authors do not release the video or audio content, only metadata and annotations.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper notes that the data relies on YouTube videos and that the authors release metadata and annotations rather than the video or audio content.

artifacts.other: The paper states that released features include Action Units from LibreFace, poses from MMDetection and MMPose, and camera angle changes from PySceneDetect.

human_samples.population: Stand-up comedians appearing in collected online stand-up comedy videos; videos with more than one comedian were excluded.
human_samples.recruitment: Videos were collected from YouTube channels listed in Table 1; YouTube Shorts and videos with more than one comedian were excluded.
human_samples.geography: Not reported at participant level; source channels include Comedy Central, Comedy Central UK, Comedy Central Latam, Comedy Central España, Comedy Central Italia, Comedy Central Magyarország, Paramount Network CZ, Montreux Comedy, and Comedy Central Brasil.
human_samples.observations: 3,617 videos; 334.2 hours; 2,887,402 words; 128,194 words labeled as laughter. These are items/labels, not participant counts.
human_samples.detail: The paper does not report unique comedian counts, demographics, or performer recruitment beyond source-channel collection and exclusions.

human_samples.population: Audiences whose laughter is audible in the stand-up comedy videos.
human_samples.recruitment: Not recruited by the study; laughter was observed in publicly available performance videos.
human_samples.observations: Audience laughter was automatically detected/refined across the corpus; Table 1 reports 128,194 words labeled as laughter, not audience members or independent laughs.
human_samples.detail: Audience identity, size, demographics, and venue information are not reported.

human_samples.population: Manual annotators for the 70-video test set; the paper says 'we manually annotate' but does not enumerate annotators.
human_samples.observations: 70 videos, 10 per language, manually annotated in laughs with timestamps at 0.1 seconds using the audio file and Audacity; ASR outputs were manually checked.
human_samples.detail: No annotator count, annotator training, adjudication procedure, blinding, or inter-annotator agreement is reported.

human_samples.population: Manual annotators for ASR-derived candidate laughter validation; the paper says the candidates were manually annotated but does not enumerate annotators.
human_samples.observations: 376 outburst candidates on 50 videos were manually annotated as real laughter or other events; 208 were real laughter events not previously detected by the off-the-shelf detector.
human_samples.detail: The paper does not report annotator count, expertise, language coverage for this subset, annotation instructions, blinding, adjudication, or agreement.

judges.name: Paper authors/research team, not individually specified
judges.detail: Manual annotations provide the labels for the 70-video laughter test set and for the 376 candidate laughter segments used to train/evaluate the Random Forest candidate validator.

judges.name: Omine et al. (2024) laughter detection model
judges.detail: Used as the off-the-shelf automatic audience-laughter detector, as the source of Raw laughter labels, and as a baseline in the IoU evaluation.

judges.name: Whisper and WhisperX timestamp comparison plus ASR-based rule
judges.detail: The proposed method uses complementary timestamp errors from Whisper and WhisperX to insert candidate laughter segments that the initial laughter detector missed.

judges.name: Random Forest candidate laughter classifier
judges.detail: Filters ASR-derived candidate laughter segments into laughter versus non-laughter/other; trained on manually labeled candidates using acoustic features.

judges.name: Precision/recall/F1, macro F1, classification metrics, and IoU threshold 0.2
judges.detail: Reported results for the candidate classifier, full laughter detection system, and word-level sequence-labeling models are determined by automatic metrics against manual or automatic labels.

generation_selection.detail: The study introduces a dataset and evaluates laughter/humor detection and sequence-labeling systems. It does not generate humorous text or select best-of-N generated outputs; ASR-derived candidate filtering is detection, not generation.

model_provenance.name: Omine et al. (2024) laughter segmentation/detection model
model_provenance.detail: Described as the state-of-the-art/off-the-shelf model used for automatic laughter detection; exact checkpoint, implementation, access method, and run date are not reported.

model_provenance.name: Whisper
model_provenance.detail: Used for transcript extraction and timestamp comparison; model size, checkpoint, implementation version, decoding settings, and run date are not reported.

model_provenance.name: WhisperX
model_provenance.detail: Used for transcript extraction and timestamp comparison; model/checkpoint version, alignment settings, and run date are not reported.

model_provenance.name: Random Forest classifier
model_provenance.detail: Trained with scikit-learn on acoustic features; reported tuned hyperparameters are n_estimators=50, max_depth=13, min_samples_split=2. Exact scikit-learn version is not reported.

model_provenance.name: xlm-roberta-base
model_provenance.detail: Accessed via transformers to fine-tune unimodal text sequence-labeling models. The paper reports transformers 4.46.3, torch 2.1.2, CUDA 12.2, and Nvidia RTX-A6000, but not a Hugging Face revision/snapshot.

model_provenance.name: LibreFace
model_provenance.detail: Used to extract Action Unit features released with the dataset; exact library/model version and run settings are not reported.

model_provenance.name: MMDetection and MMPose
model_provenance.detail: Used to extract pose features released with the dataset; exact versions/checkpoints and run settings are not reported.

model_provenance.name: PySceneDetect
model_provenance.detail: Used to extract camera angle changes released with the dataset; exact version and settings are not reported.

inference_budgets.scope: Corpus transcription and initial laughter detection
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: For each sample/video, the paper reports using two ASR systems, Whisper and WhisperX; it also reports running the Omine et al. (2024) laughter detection model on the videos.
inference_budgets.detail: No ASR model size, decoding parameters, batching, hardware, runtime, or cost are reported for this stage.

inference_budgets.scope: ASR-based candidate laughter extraction and validation
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 376 outburst candidates on 50 videos; 208 were manually labeled as real laughter. The paper states the overall method detects approximately 3 new outbursts of laughter per video.
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Segments shorter than 0.5 seconds were discarded and classified as 'other' before classifier evaluation.
inference_budgets.detail: Candidate generation depends on intersections between Whisper and WhisperX timestamp errors around laughter-like events; exact per-video candidate distribution and runtime are not reported.

inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: Binary classification over ASR-derived candidate segments.
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: 15% of the dataset was randomly selected for hyperparameter tuning/validation; tuned parameters were n_estimators=50, max_depth=13, min_samples_split=2; 200 iterations varied the training/testing split while consistently using 15% validation.
inference_budgets.detail: The paper does not report exact train/test split proportions, random seeds, elapsed time, or cost.

inference_budgets.token_budget: Maximum sequence length 512 with a stride/'stripe' of 128 when cutting from the same monologue.
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Fine-tuned xlm-roberta-base with Adam, 10 epochs, learning rate 1e-5.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Experiments used torch 2.1.2, transformers 4.46.3, a Nvidia RTX-A6000 GPU, and CUDA 12.2; cost and runtime are not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: The paper does not report batch size, random seeds, number of runs for transformer models, or inference-time settings.

Action Units, poses, and camera angle changes were extracted with LibreFace, MMDetection/MMPose, and PySceneDetect, but these features were not used in the reported baseline experiments and detailed extraction budgets are not reported.

human_baselines.scope: Laughter detection and word-level humor/laughter prediction
human_baselines.description: No human prediction baseline is reported. Human/manual annotations are used as ground truth or validation labels, not as a competing baseline system.
human_baselines.detail: No matched human-vs-model task in which humans predicted laughter from the same inputs under comparable conditions is described.

mitigations: The StandUp4AI dataset and its manual test annotations are newly introduced in the paper rather than reused as an older public benchmark.
The 70-video test set was manually annotated and ASR outputs were manually checked.
contamination_risk.basis: The paper does not discuss contamination or decontamination. Although the benchmark annotations are new, the source material consists of public YouTube videos from well-known channels, and collection/publication dates for individual videos are not reported. Pretrained components such as xlm-roberta-base, Whisper/WhisperX, and the off-the-shelf laughter detector could in principle have been exposed to related public web/audio content or transcripts. Because labels are newly created but source content is public and no decontamination test is reported, the risk is assessed as moderate.

llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: Manual annotations validate the laughter detector and the sequence-labeling test set; no LLM evaluator is used.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: The paper uses ASR systems and pretrained/fine-tuned models, but no LLM is used to judge outputs or determine headline conclusions.

reporting_gaps: Manual annotator count, annotator identities beyond 'we', annotator training, blinding, adjudication, and inter-annotator agreement are not reported.
Unique comedian counts, audience counts, and performer/audience demographics are not reported; video counts and laughter labels should not be read as participant counts.
Exact video collection dates and source video publication dates are not reported.
Exact train/dev/test split sizes for the sequence-labeling experiments are not fully specified beyond the 70-video manually annotated test set and automatic-test tables.
The Random Forest experiment reports 15% validation and 200 varied train/test splits, but not exact train/test split proportions or random seeds.
Exact checkpoints/model sizes/versions for Whisper, WhisperX, and the Omine et al. (2024) detector are not reported.
The Hugging Face revision/snapshot for xlm-roberta-base is not reported.
ASR decoding settings, batching, runtime, hardware, inference dates, and cost are not reported for Whisper/WhisperX or the Omine detector.
Transformer fine-tuning omits batch size, random seeds, number of runs, variance/confidence intervals, and inference-time settings.
Versions/checkpoints/settings for LibreFace, MMDetection, MMPose, and PySceneDetect feature extraction are not reported.
No contamination/decontamination analysis is reported for public YouTube source material or pretrained models.
No human baseline for laughter prediction or sequence labeling is reported.
